source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5419__Nebraska-Power-Review-Board/FERC RM26-4-000 (ANOPR on Interconnection of Large Loads) Nebraska Power Review Board Comments.txt
overall_summary: Nebraska Power Review Board opposes FERC assumption of jurisdiction over large load interconnection, arguing that SPP's existing HILL process already addresses the issue adequately and that federal rules would preempt state retail jurisdiction and impose uniform standards inappropriately. NPRB supports standardized deposits and readiness requirements but only at the local/RTO level, and urges FERC to raise the threshold from 20 MW to 50 MW to focus on truly large loads.

quotes.text: recent actions taken by the Southwest Power Pool (SPP) to address this issue mitigate the need for FERC's intervention
quotes.concern: SPP actions already mitigate need for federal intervention

quotes.text: The HILL process was designed to expedite the interconnection of large loads taking firm transmission service when there is sufficient generation on the system today.
quotes.concern: SPP HILL process expedites interconnection with existing generation

quotes.text: the Secretary's requested reforms are unnecessary. The necessary steps to ensure the goal of timely, orderly and nondiscriminatory interconnection of new large loads are already being taken, and are expected to be accomplished in the SPP's operating area without the need for FERC's intervention.
quotes.concern: SPP reforms are already accomplishing interconnection goals without FERC

quotes.text: the ANOPR expands FERC's jurisdiction into an area that has historically been reserved to the states. States have jurisdiction over retail sales of electric energy.
quotes.concern: FERC expansion preempts historical state retail jurisdiction

quotes.text: the provision of electric service directly to customers, including large loads, is inherently retail service
quotes.concern: Large load service is fundamentally retail, not transmission matter

quotes.text: It is not clear to the NPRB if it is anticipated that the large loads targeted in the ANOPR would be expected to receive service directly from bulk transmission lines such as 345 kV, or if FERC's jurisdiction would extend to large loads receiving service directly from 115 kV or 34.5 kV transmission and subtransmission lines.
quotes.concern: Ambiguity about voltage levels that would trigger FERC jurisdiction

quotes.text: State agencies with jurisdiction over such retail electric service have a greater understanding of the particular situation in which utilities in that state operate.
quotes.concern: States have better local knowledge for retail service decisions

quotes.text: If FERC were to assume jurisdiction over interconnection of large loads without the detailed knowledge of the local transmission, distribution, and generation facility situation, it could create strains on the local transmission system.
quotes.concern: FERC lacks local knowledge needed to preserve system reliability

quotes.text: Local electric consumers should not be required to subsidize the network upgrades and interconnection costs of new large loads.
quotes.concern: Costs must not be shifted to existing ratepayers

quotes.text: the NPRB agrees with the Secretary of Energy that standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties are needed when dealing with the interconnection of proposed large loads, the NPRB believes these solutions are best addressed at the local level, not through a standardized set of rules for all types of loads in all areas of the country.
quotes.concern: Supports deposits/readiness/penalties but via local/RTO processes, not federal rules

quotes.text: FERC should not become involved in the type of local decisions necessary for the interconnection of large loads.
quotes.concern: Local interconnection decisions should remain outside FERC scope

quotes.text: In areas with an RTO that have already developed adequate procedures, the NPRB encourages FERC to adopt a collaborative approach where FERC, state commissions and transmission providers work together to develop solutions.
quotes.concern: FERC should collaborate with RTOs and states, not impose rules unilaterally

quotes.text: SPP has been proactive in the development of such procedures and should not have to develop an entirely new process designed as a one-size-fits-all approach that may provide only marginal differences from what SPP has developed and already filed with FERC.
quotes.concern: SPP should not be forced to rebuild process with only marginal changes

quotes.text: FERC can achieve the objectives sought by the Secretary of Energy in the ANOPR without intruding into the states' area of retail jurisdiction.
quotes.concern: FERC can meet DOE goals through collaborative means without overreach

quotes.text: the 20 megawatt threshold in the ANOPR is too low. Such a small number drastically increases the number of entities that would be covered by the proposed rule.
quotes.concern: 20 MW threshold captures too many non-transmission-connected loads

quotes.text: many loads between 20 and 50 megawatts are not directly connected to the transmission system but rather are connected to distribution and sub-transmission system facilities that are not subject to FERC jurisdiction.
quotes.concern: Loads 20-50 MW often connect to subtransmission, outside FERC scope

quotes.text: By increasing the threshold in any final rule to 50 megawatts the truly large loads that have the greatest impact on the transmission system would be covered.
quotes.concern: 50 MW threshold would focus on truly large transmission-connected loads

quotes.text: for loads as small as 20 megawatts, one size does not fit all
quotes.concern: Uniform federal rules do not fit diverse load characteristics

bins.name: Oppose FERC jurisdiction over retail service
bins.description: Large load service is inherently retail service, historically reserved to states. FERC assumes would preempt state jurisdiction and lack the local knowledge needed to preserve reliability. States and local RTOs understand the particular transmission, distribution, and generation situation better than a federal regulator.

bins.name: SPP HILL process already adequately addresses interconnection
bins.description: Recent SPP actions and the HILL process mitigate the need for FERC intervention. The HILL process expedites interconnection of large loads with firm transmission service when generation exists today. SPP has already filed its procedures with FERC and should not be forced to redevelop them under a federal one-size-fits-all mandate.

bins.name: Support deposits/readiness locally, not federally
bins.description: Agrees that standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties are needed, but these solutions are best addressed at the local level through RTOs and state commissions, not through uniform federal rules applied to all loads in all areas.

bins.name: Raise minimum threshold from 20 MW to 50 MW
bins.description: The 20 MW threshold is too low and drastically increases covered entities. Many loads in the 20 to 50 MW range connect to distribution or subtransmission, not bulk transmission, and are outside FERC jurisdiction. A 50 MW threshold would focus on truly large loads with greatest transmission impact.

bins.name: Costs must not shift to local ratepayers
bins.description: Local electric consumers should not be required to subsidize network upgrades and interconnection costs of new large loads. State regulators must ensure that costs directly associated with local transmission upgrades are paid by the entity installing the large load.

bins.name: SPP region already has adequate processes
bins.description: Nebraska is entirely within SPP operating area, which has already developed streamlined HILL and CHILL processes through collaborative engagement of utilities, state regulators, developers, and board members. SPP has filed tariff revisions with FERC and should not need to duplicate those efforts under federal rules.

bins.name: Ambiguity and concern over voltage thresholds
bins.description: Unclear which voltage levels FERC would cover. Loads may receive service from 345 kV bulk transmission, 115 kV, or 34.5 kV subtransmission. The lack of clarity creates uncertainty about scope and whether federal rules would inappropriately cover subtransmission-level loads historically outside FERC jurisdiction.

bins.name: Risk that federal rules could harm local reliability
bins.description: FERC jurisdiction without detailed local knowledge of transmission, distribution, and generation could create strains on the local transmission system, particularly if generation is insufficient to handle new large loads and rapid interconnection is pursued without adequate RTO studies.

bins.name: Support local/RTO processes over federal rules
bins.description: Solutions to interconnection challenges are best addressed at the local level by RTOs and state commissions. FERC should adopt a collaborative approach with states and transmission providers, not impose one-size-fits-all rules designed at the federal level.

bins.name: FERC should collaborate rather than regulate
bins.description: FERC can achieve DOE's objectives through collaboration with state commissions and transmission providers in areas where RTOs have already developed adequate procedures. Rather than exerting jurisdiction, FERC should consider alternative remedies to encourage recalcitrant providers to implement standards.

bins.name: One-size-fits-all federal rules inappropriate
bins.description: Uniform federal rules do not fit the diversity of load characteristics and local transmission contexts across the country. Different areas have different needs, and federal standards designed for all types of loads in all regions are inappropriate.
